title: ChatGPT — Lazy prompt vs. detailed prompt
description: Same ask, two prompts. First is a one-liner and gets generic AI slop — clichés, emoji, hashtag spam, no voice. The second loads the request with audience, point of view, a specific anecdote and a forbidden-phrases list, and the output is a post that actually sounds like a person. The contrast is the lesson.

events.content: Write a LinkedIn post about hiring great people.

🎯 **Your team is your greatest asset — and hiring is the most important thing you'll ever do.**

In today's competitive landscape, the companies that win are the ones that attract, develop, and retain world-class talent. Hiring great people isn't just a function of HR — it's the single biggest lever a leader has.

Here's what I've learned about building A-player teams:

✅ **Hire for culture fit** — skills can be taught, values can't
✅ **A-players hire A-players** — B-players hire C-players
✅ **Move slow to hire, fast to fire** — protect your culture at all costs
✅ **Look for grit and growth mindset** — raw talent isn't enough
✅ **Sell the mission** — great people want meaningful work, not just a paycheck

The businesses that get hiring right will outperform their competition 10x. Don't compromise. Don't settle. Build the team of your dreams! 💪

What's your #1 hiring tip? Drop it in the comments below — would love to learn from this amazing community. 👇

#Leadership #Hiring #Talent #Culture #Entrepreneurship #Recruitment #TeamBuilding #FutureOfWork #People #Growth

That's unusable — it reads like every other AI post on LinkedIn. Try again, with proper context:

**Audience:** SME founders, 10–50 staff. Have done a bit of hiring but mostly from within their network.

**Who I am:** I run a 30-person outdoor brand. Last year I hired a head of marketing on £65k. Fifteen years' experience at brands ten times our size. Let them go after four months. Total cost of the mistake, including severance and recruitment fees: £28k.

**Voice:** First person. Dry. Short sentences. Full stops. Slightly self-deprecating. No rhetorical questions.

**Angle:** Don't write about "hiring great people" in the abstract. The specific lesson is: experience at a much bigger company isn't the same as being good at your size. At 30 people the job is doing, not directing — and a lot of senior hires don't actually want to do the work themselves anymore.

**Never use:** A-players, rockstar, ninja, culture fit, "move slow to hire fast to fire", synergy, grit, growth mindset, emojis, "in today's", "the future of", hashtag spam, "DM me". No CTA. No "what's your #1 tip" question at the end.

**Don't use em dashes!** They're the single biggest tell that something was written by AI. Use full stops and short sentences instead.

**Length:** 120 words max. One concrete takeaway at the end, no fluff.

Last year I hired a head of marketing. £65k salary. Fifteen years at brands ten times our size.

I let them go after four months.

They were excellent on strategy. Great decks, clear frameworks, sensible quarterly plans. But at 30 people there's nobody to delegate to. The campaign doesn't happen unless you write the first draft yourself. They hadn't done that in a decade.

Total cost of that mistake, with severance and recruitment fees: £28,000.

What I learned, the expensive way:

Experience at a much bigger company isn't the same as being good at your size. At 30 people the job is doing, not directing. Now I ask senior candidates one question early: *walk me through the last campaign you wrote the first draft of yourself.* The answer tells me which job they actually want.

The first prompt gave me a topic. The second gave me enough to write *as you*:

1. **A specific story, with numbers.** "£65k head of marketing, 15 years at bigger brands, let go after 4 months, £28k total cost" is something I couldn't have invented. It's the spine of the whole post.
2. **A point of view.** "Experience at a bigger company isn't the same as being good at your size" is an argument, not a subject. The lazy prompt asked for a topic. This one gave me a thesis to defend.
3. **A voice.** "Dry, slightly self-deprecating, short sentences, no rhetorical questions" told me how to sound. Without it I default to the LinkedIn house style. That's why generic AI posts all read the same.
4. **A forbidden list.** "No A-players, rockstar, culture fit, grit, growth mindset, em dashes, emojis, hashtags" stripped out the reflexes I'd otherwise reach for. The em dash ban alone does a lot of work: it's the clearest fingerprint that something was written by AI, and removing it forces shorter sentences.
5. **A length cap.** 120 words forced every sentence to carry weight. The first post was 210 words mostly saying nothing.

The pattern, more generally: the lazy prompt asks for *a thing*. The good prompt hands over context, opinions, and specifics the model couldn't have guessed, then trusts it to write.
